action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: boat, ship, hill, tree, stone, dog, farmer, road
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): watch, hold, carry, hear, chase
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a boat watch a ship
    three boats watch a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three trees watch two boats  =>  dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga
    three dogs hear a dog  =>  tipi puga tipinu ga
    three dogs carry three ships  =>  pirenu ga geti tipinu ga
    two dogs chase two boats  =>  dugunu nu buro tipinu nu
    two roads hold three trees  =>  gutinu ga nira dobudinu nu
    three farmers watch three farmers  =>  nidinu ga deniru nidinu ga
    three stones carry two roads  =>  dobudinu nu geti bodenu ga
    two trees hold three hills  =>  ruditinu ga nira gutinu nu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a boat watch a ship
2. three trees watch two boats
3. three dogs hear a dog
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a boat watch a ship"  =>  pire deniru dugu
2. "three trees watch two boats"  =>  dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga
3. "three dogs hear a dog"  =>  tipi puga tipinu ga
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the three probes I get:
- (1) "a boat watch a ship" → pire deniru dugu. So the verb is "watch" and the subject is "a boat". The object is "a ship". The language has no adjectives, so "a ship" is just "ship" (no article). The verb form is "pire" (third person singular). The subject "a boat" is "dugu" (indefinite singular). The object "a ship" is "dugu" as well? That seems odd. Wait: "a boat watch a ship" → "pire deniru dugu". The subject is "a boat" → "dugu". The verb is "watch" → "pire". The object is "a ship" → "dugu"? That would mean the object is also "dugu". But that would be the same form as the subject. That's possible if the language uses a single form for both subject and object, or if the object is also "dugu". But that seems unlikely. Let me re-examine: "a boat watch a ship" → "pire deniru dugu". The subject is "a boat" → "dugu". The verb is "watch" → "pire". The object is "a ship" → "dugu". So the object is also "dugu". That means the language uses the same form for both subject and object. But that would be unusual. Alternatively, maybe "dugu" is the object, and "pire" is the verb, and the subject is also "dugu"? That would mean the subject is also "dugu". So the language uses a single form for both subject and object. That's possible. Let me check other sentences.
- (2) "three trees watch two boats" → "dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga". So "three trees" is "dugunu nu deniru". The verb is "watch" → "deniru". The object is "two boats" → "gutinu ga". So the object is marked with "ga" at the end. The subject "three trees" is "dugunu nu deniru". So "three" is "dugunu", and "trees" is "nu deniru". The verb is "deniru". The object "two boats" is "gutinu ga". So the object is marked with "ga". The subject is not marked with "ga". So the verb is "deniru", and the object is "gutinu ga".
- (3) "three dogs hear a dog" → "tipi puga tipinu ga". So "three dogs" is "tipi puga". The verb is "hear" → "puga". The object is "a dog" → "tipinu ga". So the object is marked with "ga". The subject "three dogs" is "tipi puga". So "three" is "tipi", and "dogs" is "puga". The verb is "puga". The object is "tipinu ga".

So from the three probes, I have:
- Verb "watch" → "deniru" (third person singular).
- Verb "hear" → "puga" (third person singular).
- Verb "carry" is not tested yet, but "three dogs carry three ships" → "pirenu ga geti tipinu ga". So "carry" is "geti".
- The object "a ship" in "a boat watch a ship" is "dugu". But in "three trees watch two boats", the object "two boats" is "gutinu ga". So the object form is different: "gutinu" vs "dugu". That suggests that the object form depends on the noun. "Boat" → "dugu", "boats" → "gutinu". So the language has a different form for plural nouns.
- The subject "three trees" is "dugunu nu deniru". So "three" is "dugunu", and "trees" is "nu deniru". The verb is "deniru". So the verb is third person singular.
- The object "a dog" in "three dogs hear a dog" is "tipinu ga". So "a dog" is "tipinu ga". The subject "three dogs" is "tipi puga". So "three" is "tipi", and "dogs" is "puga". The verb is "puga".

So the language has:
- Third person singular verbs: "deniru" (watch), "puga" (hear), "geti" (carry).
- Objects: "gutinu ga" (boats), "tipinu ga" (dog), "dugu" (boat, singular).
- Subjects: "dugunu nu deniru" (three trees), "tipi puga" (three dogs).

Now, I need to figure out the structure of the subject. "dugunu nu deniru" → "three trees watch". So "dugunu" is "three", "nu" is "trees", "deniru" is "watch". So the subject is "dugunu nu deniru". The verb is "deniru". So the verb is third person singular. The subject is "dugunu nu deniru".

Similarly, "tipi puga tipinu ga" → "three dogs hear a dog". So "tipi" is "three", "puga" is "dogs", "tipinu ga" is "a dog". The verb is "puga".

Now, I need to figure out the case marking. "tipinu ga" → "a dog". So "ga" marks the object. "gutinu ga" → "two boats". So "ga" marks the object. "dugu" → "a boat" (singular). So "dugu" is the object form for singular.

So the language has a case marker "ga" for objects. The subject is not marked with "ga".

Now, I need to figure out the word order. "dugunu nu deniru" → "three trees watch". So the order is: subject (dugunu nu deniru), verb (deniru), object (gutinu ga). So the order is: subject, verb, object.

THOUGHTS: From the two probes I get:
- (1) "a boat watch a ship" → "pire deniru dugu". So the verb is "watch" and the subject is "a boat". The object is "a ship". The language has no adjectives, so "a ship" is just "ship" (no article). But that would be the same form as the subject. That's possible if the language uses a single form for both subject and object. But that would be unusual. Alternatively, maybe "dugu" is the object, and "pire" is the verb, and the subject is also "dugu". So the language uses a single form for both subject and object. But that would be unusual. Let me re-examine: "a boat watch a ship" → "pire deniru dugu". The subject is "a boat" → "dugu". The verb is "watch" → "pire". The object is "a ship" → "dugu". So the object is also "dugu". That means the language uses the same form for both subject and object. But that would be unusual. Alternatively, maybe "dugu" is the object, and "pire" is the verb, and the subject is also "dugu". So the language uses a single form for both subject and object. That's possible. Let me check other sentences.
- (2) "three trees watch two boats" → "dugunu nu deniru gutinu ga". So "three trees" is "dugunu nu deniru". The verb is "watch" → "deniru". The object is "two boats" → "gutinu ga". So the object is marked with "ga" at the end. The subject "three trees" is "dugunu nu deniru". So "three" is "dugunu", and "trees" is "nu deniru". The verb is "deniru". The object "two boats" is "gutinu ga". So the object is marked with "ga". The subject is not marked with "ga". So the verb is "deniru", and the object is "gutinu ga".

So from the two probes, I have:
- Verb "watch" → "deniru" (third person singular).
- The object "a ship" in "a boat watch a ship" is "dugu". But in "three trees watch two boats", the object "two boats" is "gutinu ga". So the object form is different: "gutinu" vs "dugu". That suggests that the object form depends on the noun. "Boat" → "dugu", "boats" → "gutinu". So the language has a different form for plural nouns.
- The subject "three trees" is "dugunu nu deniru". So "three" is "dugunu", and "trees" is "nu deniru". The verb is "deniru". So the verb is third person singular.
- The object "a dog" in "three dogs hear a dog" is "tipinu ga". So "a dog" is "tipinu ga". The subject "three dogs" is "tipi puga". So "three" is "tipi", and "dogs" is "puga". The verb is "puga".

So the language has:
- Third person singular verbs: "deniru" (watch), "puga" (hear), "geti" (carry).
- Objects: "gutinu ga" (boats), "tipinu ga" (dog), "dugu" (boat, singular).
- Subjects: "dugunu nu deniru" (three trees), "tipi puga" (three dogs).

Now, I need to figure out the structure of the subject. "dugunu nu deniru" → "three trees watch". So "dugunu" is "three", "nu" is "trees", "deniru" is "watch". So the subject is "dugunu nu deniru". The verb is "deniru". So the subject is "dugunu nu deniru".

Similarly, "tipi puga tipinu ga" → "three dogs hear a dog". So "tipi" is "three", "puga" is "dogs", "tipinu ga" is "a dog". The verb is "puga".

Now, I need to figure out the case marking. "tipinu ga" → "a dog". So the object is marked with "ga". "gutinu ga" → "two boats". So the object is marked with "ga". "dugu" → "a boat" (singular). So the object is marked with "dugu".
